description: A seed case opens along its seam, firewood parts with its grain, or a path divides into branches. 'pisha' follows a line of parting already offered by the thing or route. It may produce two parts or several; 'kati' (cut) instead centres the edge that makes the division.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release them into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Raise the tongue blade towards the postalveolar area for unvoiced 'sh', send air through the central groove, and lower the jaw into open central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: The word begins behind closed lips, slips into the longer friction of 'sh', and opens on 'a'. It feels less like a blow than a seam giving way.
usage_notes: The verb appears intransitively when a seed, path, or material divides. A direct object can name material deliberately split by a person or tool; the event-noun rule supplies a split or fork.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Splitting wood well begins with the grain and the existing checks in the timber. The verb keeps that cooperation with material distinct from cutting across it.

semantic_domains.activity: deliberately parting material along a line it readily follows
semantic_domains.nature: seeds, branches, and other living forms opening or dividing
semantic_domains.physical: one body or route separating into multiple parts
